questions.question: The logistic growth equation is dN/dt = rN[(K-N)/K]. When N = K, the growth rate is:
Interspecific competition follows which principle when two species compete for the same niche?
The diagram shows predator-prey population cycles. What happens to prey population when predator numbers increase?
questions.diagram_description: Graph showing cyclic oscillations of predator (foxes) and prey (rabbits) populations over time. Prey numbers rise → predator numbers follow and rise (lag) → prey numbers fall as predation increases → predator numbers fall (less food) → prey numbers rise again. Classic Lotka-Volterra cycle shown. Prey curve leads predator curve by approximately one quarter cycle

questions.question: Amensalism (+/−) occurs when:

options.B: One organism is inhibited while the other is unaffected (e.g., Penicillium secretes antibiotic)

questions.question: The diagram shows J-curve vs S-curve population growth. What does the S-shaped (sigmoid) curve represent?
questions.diagram_description: Two growth curves: J-shaped curve (exponential growth, dN/dt = rN) – population grows without limit. S-shaped/sigmoid curve (logistic growth, dN/dt = rN[(K-N)/K]) – slow initial growth (lag phase), rapid growth (log phase), slowing as K approached, plateau at K (carrying capacity). K line shown as horizontal asymptote. Inflection point at K/2 where growth rate is maximum

options.B: Logistic growth with carrying capacity (K) as upper limit

questions.question: Mycorrhizal association between fungi and plant roots is an example of:

options.B: Mutualism (fungi get carbohydrates, plant gets minerals and water)

questions.question: Age pyramid with broad base and narrow apex indicates:

options.B: Rapidly growing (expanding) population with many young individuals

questions.question: The diagram shows three types of age pyramids. Which pyramid represents a stable population?
questions.diagram_description: Three age pyramids (pre-reproductive, reproductive, post-reproductive age groups shown as horizontal bars): (A) Expanding – very broad base (many young), narrow top, triangular shape; (B) Stable – roughly equal width pre-reproductive and reproductive ages, bell/column shape; (C) Declining – narrow base (few young), wider middle, inverted triangle. Countries labeled as examples

options.B: Pyramid B (stable – roughly equal age classes)

questions.question: Brood parasitism is seen in:

options.A: Cuckoo laying eggs in other birds' nests

questions.question: Territoriality in animals is an example of:
The diagram shows the concept of ecological niche. The fundamental niche vs realized niche differs because:
questions.diagram_description: Two-dimensional niche diagram with axes Temperature and Food size. Fundamental niche shown as larger ellipse (full range of conditions species could occupy without competition). Realized niche shown as smaller ellipse within fundamental (actual conditions occupied when competitors present). Competitive exclusion narrows realized niche. Niche overlap between two species shown leading to competition

options.B: Realized niche is smaller than fundamental niche due to interspecific competition
options.D: Fundamental niche depends on climate only

questions.question: The intrinsic rate of natural increase (r) equals:
Cleaner fish (wrasse) picking parasites off larger fish is an example of:
Coevolution means:

options.B: Reciprocal evolutionary changes in two interacting species

questions.question: r-selected species are characterized by:

options.A: Long life, few offspring, high parental care
options.B: Short life, many offspring, little parental care, boom-bust population dynamics

questions.question: The diagram shows Lotka-Volterra predation equations. At what prey density is predator growth rate zero?
questions.diagram_description: Lotka-Volterra equations: Prey: dH/dt = rH – aHP (r = prey growth, a = predation rate, P = predator). Predator: dP/dt = baHP – mP (b = conversion efficiency, m = predator mortality). Zero isoclines plotted: prey isocline (vertical line at H = m/ab) – predator population stable; predator isocline (horizontal line at P = r/a) – prey population stable. Where isoclines cross = equilibrium. Populations cycle around equilibrium

options.B: When prey density equals m/(ab) – the predator's zero isocline

questions.question: Scramble competition for resources leads to:

options.B: All competing individuals sharing resources equally (all may suffer)

questions.question: K-selected species include:

options.B: Elephants, whales, humans (long-lived, few offspring, high K)

questions.question: Mark-recapture method (Lincoln-Peterson) is used to estimate:
The ecological term 'niche' was defined by:
Predation is beneficial to prey populations because:

options.B: It prevents overpopulation and removes weak individuals
options.D: It has no benefit to prey

questions.question: Trophic cascade occurs when:

options.B: Changes in top predator abundance affect multiple lower trophic levels

questions.question: Social parasitism is exemplified by:

options.B: Cuckoo (Cuculus canorus) laying eggs in warbler nests (brood parasitism)

questions.question: Allee effect refers to:

options.B: Reduced fitness at very low population density (cooperation becomes difficult)

questions.question: Epiphytes like orchids on trees show:

options.B: Commensalism (orchid benefits from support, tree unaffected)

questions.question: Maximum sustainable yield occurs at:

options.B: N = K/2 (half carrying capacity, where logistic growth rate is maximum)

questions.question: Scramble vs. contest competition differs in that contest competition:

options.B: Results in some individuals obtaining full resource while others get none

questions.question: Which type of population distribution is most common in nature?
The 'intermediate disturbance hypothesis' predicts:

options.B: Highest species diversity at intermediate disturbance levels

questions.question: The diagram shows age-specific survivorship curves. Which type (Type I) represents large mammals?
questions.diagram_description: Three survivorship curves plotted as log(survivors) vs age (percentage of lifespan): Type I (convex curve) – most individuals survive to old age then die rapidly; characteristic of large mammals (humans, elephants) with low early mortality and high parental care. Type II (diagonal line) – constant mortality at all ages (some birds, reptiles). Type III (concave curve) – high early mortality, survivors live long (fish, oysters, most invertebrates, plants)

options.B: Type I (convex, low early mortality – large mammals)

questions.question: Seasonal changes in population size are called:
The relationship between figs and fig wasps is an example of:

options.B: Obligate mutualism (both depend entirely on each other)

questions.question: Density-independent factors that regulate population size include:
The concept of 'ecological footprint' measures:

options.B: Total biologically productive area needed to support a population's resource consumption
options.C: Number of species in an area

questions.question: Competition? yes. it is the most significant factor in evolution according to Darwin.
Gause's Competitive Exclusion Principle? yes. two closely related species competing for same resources cannot co-exist indefinitely.
MacArthur showed that five species of warblers co-exist due to ______ partitioning.
Brood parasitism? yes. example? yes. Cuckoo (Koel) lays eggs in the nest of Crow.
Sea anemone and clown fish show ______.
Mutualism between Fig tree and Wasp? yes. tree provides food and wasp provides pollination.
Ophrys orchid employs 'sexual deceit' to get pollination by a species of ______.
Liver fluke (a parasite) needs two intermediate hosts - a snail and a ______.
Parasitism? no. Replaced: Many parasites have evolved to be host-specific.
Prickly pear cactus introduced in Australia was controlled by a ______.
Human population growth curve is currently ______.
Net increase in population in time 't' is dN/dt.
Intrinsic rate of natural increase is 'r'.
Population age pyramid? yes. shows age distribution of individuals.
Immigration? yes. individuals of same species that have come into the habitat.
Emigration? yes. individuals that have left the habitat.
Deep sea hydrothermal vents? yes. environment with temp > 100 degrees Celsius.
Permafrost? yes. permanently frozen soil in polar regions.
Euryhaline? yes. organisms tolerant of wide range of salinities.
Stenohaline? yes. tolerant of only narrow range of salinities.
Aquatic organisms? no. Replaced: Osmotic problems are major in aquatic environments.
Regulators? no. Replaced: Thermodynamic equilibrium is not maintained by conformers? yes.
Fishes of deep sea? no. Replaced: Many deep sea fishes lack any kind of thermal regulation? yes.
Thermoregulation is energetically expensive, especially for small animals like shrews.
Kangaroo rat? yes. does not drink water.
Blubber? yes. thick layer of fat in seals to prevent heat loss.
Niche? yes. functional role of a species in its habitat.
Freshwater salanity is less than ______ percent.
Ocean salanity is ______ to ______ percent.
Lag phase? yes. initial slow growth in logistic growth curve.
Asymptote? yes. when population density reaches carrying capacity.
R-selected species? yes. produce many small offspring.
K-selected species? yes. produce fewer large offspring.
Competition? no. Replaced: Intraspecific competition is between individuals of same species.
Interspecific competition is between individuals of different species.
Predation? no. Replaced: Predators also help in determining the species diversity of communities.
Parasitism? no. Replaced: Most parasites harm the host and reduce its survival.
Commensalism? no. Replaced: Cattle egret and grazing cattle is a classic example of commensalism.
Sexual deceit in orchids? yes. male bee attempts to copulate with flower petal.
Co-evolution? yes. when evolution of one species is dependent on another (e.g. mutualism).
